## Highlights

- We propose the use of a magnetoresponsive stem cell spheroid (MR-SCS)-based cartilage recovery platform that allows for precise targeting using an electromagnetic actuation system to provide magnetic control and low-frequency electromagnetic field to allow for biophysical stimulation to promote chondrogenic differentiation.

- Locomotion of MR-SCS that was mediated by the electromagnetic actuationsystem was successfully demonstrated in 3D phantom and ex vivomodels.

• Histological evaluation revealed an apparent improvement in the regeneration of cartilage tissue in an ex vivomodel of the porcine femur in response to Low-frequency electromagnetic field stimulation.
Abstract

Mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) provide a promising source for cartilage tissue regeneration strategies. The use of MSCs for such strategies, however, remains challenging due to the low targeting and low chondrogenic differentiation efficiency of these cells to the desired site. In an attempt to overcome such problems, we propose the use of a magnetoresponsive stem cell spheroid (MR-SCS)-based cartilage recovery platform that allows for precise targeting using an electromagnetic actuation (EMA) system to provide magnetic control and low-frequency electromagnetic field (LF-EMF) to allow for biophysical stimulation to promote chondrogenic differentiation. MR-SCSs were fabricated from mouse-derived MSCs that were labeled with magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) using 3D culture methods, and these particles exhibited no cytotoxicity and did not affect chondrogenic differentiation. Locomotion of MR-SCS that was mediated by the EMA system was successfully demonstrated in 3D phantom and ex vivo models. Additionally, LF-EMF stimulation of MR-SCS resulted in increased expression levels of cartilage specific markers, collagen type II, SOX9, and Aggrecan. Finally, histological evaluation revealed an apparent improvement in the regeneration of cartilage tissue in an ex vivo model of the porcine femur in response to LF-EMF stimulation. These results suggest that our experimental platform consisting of MR-SCSs that are subjected to EMA and EMF stimulation may provide a promising therapeutic system for cartilage tissue regeneration.
Keywords: stem cell, cell spheroid, electromagnetic actuation, electromagnetic field stimulation

## Abstract

Mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) provide a promising source for cartilage tissue regeneration strategies. The use of MSCs for such strategies, however, remains challenging due to the low targeting and low chondrogenic differentiation efficiency of these cells to the desired site. In an attempt to overcome such problems, we propose the use of a magnetoresponsive stem cell spheroid (MR-SCS)-based cartilage recovery platform that allows for precise targeting using an electromagnetic actuation (EMA) system to provide magnetic control and low-frequency electromagnetic field (LF-EMF) to allow for biophysical stimulation to promote chondrogenic differentiation. MR-SCSs were fabricated from mouse-derived MSCs that were labeled with magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) using 3D culture methods, and these particles exhibited no cytotoxicity and did not affect chondrogenic differentiation. Locomotion of MR-SCS that was mediated by the EMA system was successfully demonstrated in 3D phantom and ex vivo models. Additionally, LF-EMF stimulation of MR-SCS resulted in increased expression levels of cartilage specific markers, collagen type II, SOX9, and Aggrecan. Finally, histological evaluation revealed an apparent improvement in the regeneration of cartilage tissue in an ex vivo model of the porcine femur in response to LF-EMF stimulation. These results suggest that our experimental platform consisting of MR-SCSs that are subjected to EMA and EMF stimulation may provide a promising therapeutic system for cartilage tissue regeneration.

Keywords: stem cell, cell spheroid, electromagnetic actuation, electromagnetic field stimulation

### 1. Introduction

parsing_res_list.block_content: Journal Pre-proof

As the world population increases in age and in life expectancy, joint related disorders such as osteoarthritis (OA) are emerging as a major issue in regard to global health. Approximately 10 % of the world population over the age of 60 suffer from OA [1]. OA is usually accompanied by degradation of articular cartilage, inflammation, thickening of the subchondral bone, and formation of osteophytes [2]. For many years, scientists have attempted to regenerate damaged cartilage in patients with OA. Articular cartilage exhibits a low capacity for self-repair, while other tissues such as bone possess the ability to self-regenerate [3]. Given the low capacity for self-repair in cartilage, several treatments have been developed to aid in the healing of injured articular cartilage. Current methods used to treat damaged cartilage include surgical techniques, such as microfracture and osteochondral grafting, and cell-based techniques, such as autologous stem cell or chondrocyte implantations. To facilitate cartilage regeneration, it is necessary to reconstruct the mature, well structured, and biomechanically compliant hyaline cartilage that is primarily composed of collagen type II (COL II) [4]. Surgical methods, however, are limited in their ability to regenerate hyaline cartilage [5]. These methods instead typically result in the regeneration of fibrocartilage that contains a higher percentage of collagen type I compared to that of collagen type II, while implantation of autologous stem cells or chondrocytes results in the generation of more hyaline-like cartilage [6]. Additionally, surgical treatments can be accompanied by many complications such as inflammation, infection, and implantation failure, and they can also result in the need for revision surgery [7, 8]. In contrast, cell-based therapy can be used to facilitate a direct injection of cells into the sites of damage without the need for surgical procedures, and this can reduce operation time and the risk of complications. Due to these advantages, cell-based therapy using mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) or chondrocytes is now the more prominent treatment for injured cartilage regeneration.

As the world population increases in age and in life expectancy, joint related disorders such as osteoarthritis (OA) are emerging as a major issue in regard to global health. Approximately 10 % of the world population over the age of 60 suffer from OA [1]. OA is usually accompanied by degradation of articular cartilage, inflammation, thickening of the subchondral bone, and formation of osteophytes [2]. For many years, scientists have attempted to regenerate damaged cartilage in patients with OA. Articular cartilage exhibits a low capacity for self-repair, while other tissues such as bone possess the ability to self-regenerate [3]. Given the low capacity for self-repair in cartilage, several treatments have been developed to aid in the healing of injured articular cartilage. Current methods used to treat damaged cartilage include surgical techniques, such as microfracture and osteochondral grafting, and cell-based techniques, such as autologous stem cell or chondrocyte implantations. To facilitate cartilage regeneration, it is necessary to reconstruct the mature, well structured, and biomechanically compliant hyaline cartilage that is primarily composed of collagen type II (COL II) [4]. Surgical methods, however, are limited in their ability to regenerate hyaline cartilage [5]. These methods instead typically result in the regeneration of fibrocartilage that contains a higher percentage of collagen type I compared to that of collagen type II, while implantation of autologous stem cells or chondrocytes results in the generation of more hyaline-like cartilage [6]. Additionally, surgical treatments can be accompanied by many complications such as inflammation, infection, and implantation failure, and they can also result in the need for revision surgery [7, 8]. In contrast, cell-based therapy can be used to facilitate a direct injection of cells into the sites of damage without the need for surgical procedures, and this can reduce operation time and the risk of complications. Due to these advantages, cell-based therapy using mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) or chondrocytes is now the more prominent treatment for injured cartilage regeneration.

MSCs provide a promising cell source for cartilage repair due to their self-renewal ability, high proliferation capability, multilineage differentiation, and self-regeneration capability for damaged

parsing_res_list.block_content: Journal Pre-proof

tissues and organs [9]. A number of studies have investigated the applicability of MSCs in regenerative medicine for the repair of minor and major cartilage defects, and these studies have reported that MSCs can maintain their properties even after several expansion passages [10]. Although MSCs possess excellent therapeutic advantages for cartilage regeneration, low efficiency of cell delivery and maintenance of these cells at the defect area after intra-articular injection continue to pose problems. Loss of cells may result in low therapeutic effects, and accumulation of these cells at inappropriate locations may result in unwanted lesions that can later become cancerous.

In an effort to enhance the targeting efficiency of stem cells, several studies have suggested the use of magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) loaded into individual stem cells that can be manipulated by external magnetic fields generated from permanent magnets [11]. Through the use of these permanent magnets, individual MSCs loaded with MNPs can be guided and concentrated at sites of damage to promote cartilage regeneration. To precisely deliver the cells into deep cartilage sites such as the meniscus at the tibia or the back side of femoral cartilage, however, magnetic control with a high degree-of-freedom is required. The electromagnetic actuation (EMA) system that is composed of multiple electromagnetic coils provides one promising method to actively control these cells [12, 13]. As the electromagnetic field strength generated by the EMA system, however, is basically lower than the magnetic field from the permanent magnet, a high enough concentration of MNPs should be loaded into the individual stem cells, and these concentrations may cause cytotoxicity.

Given this, the proof of concept of a three-dimensional (3D) scaffold-based stem cell delivery system that is magnetically controlled by an EMA system for cartilage regeneration has been suggested [12]. According to the results of this study, the use of poly(lactic-co-glycolic acid) (PLGA) scaffolds with MNPs improved the efficiency of cell delivery to damaged sites and increased the healing effects of this treatment in regard to cartilage defects. still some unsolved issues associated with long-term safety and efficacy related to these materials. Scaffolds created using synthetic polymers may exhibit degradation problems, and scaffolds made with natural polymers may cause immunological issues or infection [14]. Additionally, the use of porous scaffolds for cell-based therapy also poses some limitations when used as an optimized delivery system. The procedure and the efficacy of micro-scaffolds that possess highly homogenous pores both tend to vary, and cell loading density tends to be inconsistent [15]. Given these limitations, scaffold-free cell spheroids may provide an excellent alternative cell delivery system due to their safety and simple manufacturing process, as these qualities could lower the unknown risks and simplify the procedures. Additionally, spheroids more accurately represent the in vivo environment that promotes intercellular interaction, and this may be beneficial for cartilage regeneration [16].

Currently, stem cell-based tissue engineering strategies that incorporate biochemical and biophysical stimulations to induce cell proliferation and differentiation in damaged tissue have attracted a great deal of attention. Biophysical factors, such as stimulation by low-frequency electromagnetic field (LF-EMF), have been reported to promote the release of adequate growth factors and to induce chondrocyte proliferation, differentiation, and proteoglycan production in damaged cartilage [17]. Additionally, LF-EMF stimulation is a non-invasive therapy that is not known to cause any side effects, allowing this technique to be applied during cell culture and under in vivo conditions [18]. Based on the finding of these previous studies, LF-EMF may provide a useful biophysical approach to promote chondrogenic differentiation and the repair of damaged articular cartilage.

2. Experimental section
2.1 Cell culture

Mouse mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) were obtained from American Type Culture Collection (ATCC, VA, USA) and cultivated in culture medium composed of Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM; Sigma-Aldrich, MO, USA), 10 % (v/v) fetal bovine serum (FBS; Sigma-Aldrich, MO, USA), and 1 % (v/v) penicillin-streptomycin (PS; Sigma-Aldrich, MO, USA) under 37 °C in a humidified 5 % CO₂ conditions. MSCs passage of 5-6 were used in the experiments and no more than MSCs passage of 7 were used in the experiments.

### 2. Experimental section

### 2.1 Cell culture

Mouse mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) were obtained from American Type Culture Collection (ATCC, VA, USA) and cultivated in culture medium composed of Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM; Sigma-Aldrich, MO, USA), 10 % (v/v) fetal bovine serum (FBS; Sigma-Aldrich, MO, USA), and 1 % (v/v) penicillin-streptomycin (PS; Sigma-Aldrich, MO, USA) under 37 °C in a humidified 5 % CO₂ conditions. The medium was replaced every 2-3 days, and cells were detached from the culture plate when they became 80% confluent using cell dissociation solution to allow for subculture and for further experiments. MSCs passage of 5-6 were used in the experiments and no more than MSCs passage of 7 were used in the experiments.

### 2.2 Fabrication and characteristics of MNP labeled MR-SCS

Confluent mouse MSCs were incubated in culture medium containing different concentrations of MNPs (fluidMAG-D, Chemicell, Germany) that ranged from 0-0.5 mg/ml at 37 °C overnight. After cells were exposed to different concentration of MNPs, washed with PBS to remove the remaining

parsing_res_list.block_content: Journal Pre-proof

MNPs. The fluidMAG-D is composed of magnetite core and starch surface coating and prepared as a concentration in suspension of 25 mg/mL. The hydrodynamic size of the fluidMAG-D is about 100 nm from the manufacturer, and the size measured from several research groups is 114 nm ± 1 nm [19, 20]. AlamarBlue Cell viability reagent (Invitrogen, OR, USA) and CellTiter-Glo® 3D cell viability assay (Promega, WI, USA) were used to evaluate mouse MSCs viability on MNPs at day 1, 3, and 7. Low-attachment surfaces techniques were used to generate the MR-SCS. MNP labeled mouse MSCs were dissociated, and 10,000 cells/well were distributed into Corning Ultra-Low Attachment Surface 96-well plates (Corning, NY, USA) and then incubated at 37 °C under humidified 5% CO₂ conditions. The size of the MR-SCS was analyzed by microscopy using ImageJ software available at http://rsb.info.nih.gov/ij/. Uptake and contents of MNP in the MR-SCS was confirmed by Prussian blue staining (Polysciences, Inc., Germany) and ICP-OES analysis. Bio-Transmission electron microscopy (BIO-TEM, JEOL Ltd, Japan) was used to confirm the location of MNPs on cells. Additionally, cell tracker (ThermoFisher, MA, USA) was used to observe morphology, and Live/Dead cell viability assay was used to confirm viability of the MR-SCS.
2.3 Chondrogenic differentiation of the MR-SCS

The fabricated MR-SCSs were cultured for 3 weeks in chondrogenic differentiation medium composed of DMEM containing 1% insulin-transferrin-selenium supplement (ITS), 0.1 μM dexamethasone, 50 μg/ml ascorbic acid, 100 μg/ml sodium pyruvate, 40 μg/ml proline, and 10 ng/ml Recombinant human transforming growth factor-beta 1 (TGF-β1) at 37°C under a humidified 5 % CO₂ atmosphere. The chondrogenic differentiation medium was changed every 2-3 days.

### 2.4 Experimental setup for electromagnetic actuation of the MR-SCS

parsing_res_list.block_content: Journal Pre-proof

The electromagnetic actuation of the MR-SCS was conducted using the EMA system that consists of eight electromagnetic coils and soft magnetic cores (see Supplementary Materials for more details). The design of the proposed EMA system was based on our previous work [12]. The EMA system can create uniform magnetic fields and gradient magnetic fields of 40 mT and 1.8 T/m, and it can also realize five degrees of freedom (DOF) in regard to magnetic objects. Detailed characterization of the EMA system is explained in the Supplementary Information. To observe the 3D locomotion of the MR-SCS, a microscope (f170, Carl Zeiss, Germany) and a DSLR camera (EOS 600D, CANON, Japan) were introduced as bi-plane imaging devices. A controller based on LabVIEW software (National Instruments, Austin, Texas, USA) and eight power supplies (each four sets of MX 15 and 3001 ix, AMETEK, USA) were used to control the input current of each coil.

For the 3D locomotion test of the MR-SCS using the EMA system, a single MR-SCS was placed in the acrylic cube with one side length of 20 mm, and this was filled with a 70% glycerol solution. For the 3D targeting test of the MR-SCS, the distal condyle was harvested from a porcine femur, and a cartilage defect was then created using a 4 mm diameter biopsy punch. The prepared ex-vivo sample was placed in the chamber with the 70% glycerol solution.
2.5 Experimental setup for low-field electromagnetic stimulation of the MR-SCS

For electromagnetic stimulation of the MR-SCS, an LF-EMF stimulation system that consists of two square coils and a portable cell incubator was developed. The coils possessed a similar configuration to a Helmholtz coil system and were designed to produce high uniform magnetic fields in the workspace, and the detailed specifications are presented in table S1. To maintain cell culture of the MR-SCS during magnetic field stimulation, the portable cell incubator was placed between the electromagnetic coils at a maintained cell culture atmosphere at 37 °C, 95% air, 5% CO₂, and 95% humidity. For electromagnetic stimulation of the MR-SCS, an LF-EMF stimulation system that consists of two square coils and a portable cell incubator was developed. The coils possessed a similar configuration to a Helmholtz coil system and were designed to produce high uniform magnetic fields in the workspace, and the detailed specifications are presented in table S1. To maintain cell culture of the MR-SCS during magnetic field stimulation, the portable cell incubator was placed between the electromagnetic coils at a maintained cell culture atmosphere at 37 °C, 95% air, 5% CO₂, and 95% humidity. In terms of parameters of LF-EMF stimulation for chondrogenic

Recent study on magnetic field stimulation with these parameters has shown that chondrogenic differentiation of MSCs is improved in collagen type II expression and glycosaminoglycan content [18]. Based on chondrogenic differentiation using the parameters of previous study for magnetic field stimulation, we exposed MR-SCS to a sinusoidal magnetic field of 5 mT and 15 Hz for 30 min per day. Bell, USA) to maintain desired magnetic field intensity.

3. Results
3.1 Morphology and cell viability of the MR-SCS

Mouse MSCs and ultra-low attachment surface 96-well plates were used to fabricate the magnetoresponsive stem cell spheroid (MR-SCS). Mouse MSCs labeled with MNPs were agglomerated together and formed into spherical shapes within 3 days. Microscopy and confocal images were used to determine the morphology of the MR-SCS (Fig. 2a, d). The images confirmed that the MR-SCS uniformly formed into spherical shapes regardless of MNP labeling. As the labeling concentrations of MNP increased, the size of the MR-SCS increased. The average size of MNP unlabeled MR-SCS was 182.5 ± 8.6 μm, and the average size of the 0.5 mg/ml MNP labeled MR-SCS was 291.3 ± 16.4 μm.

The effect of MNPs on the viability of mouse MSCs in 2D and 3D culture was analyzed using the AlamarBlue assay, and the range of MNP labeling concentrations was 0-0.5 mg/ml on day 1, 3, and 7. The data obtained for this experiment indicated that the viability of mouse MSC labeled with MNPs up to 0.2 mg/ml in both 2D and 3D culture did not decrease to less than 90% compared to the viability of unlabeled mouse MSCs (control) at day 1, 3, and 7 (Fig. 2b, fig. S1). Cytotoxicity was observed when MNPs were used at concentrations of greater than 0.3 mg/ml in both 2D and 3D culture of mouse MSCs. These data indicated that labeling concentration of less than 0.2 mg/ml of MNPs is biocompatible with mouse MSCs. Thus, less than 0.2 mg/ml of MNP was used as the cell labeling concentration for further experiments. The number of cells per 0.2 mg/ml MNP labeled spheroid were confirmed by with Alamar blue cell viability reagent and CellTiter-Glo® 3D cell viability reagent. Initial seeding number of cells per MNP labeled spheroid was about  $ 1.2 \times 10^{4} $.

### 3.3 Manipulation of the MR-SCS using EMA system

The electromagnetic actuation of the MR-SCS was performed using the EMA system, as this system can provide an untethered motion to the magnetic objects (Fig. 3a) [12]. The proposed EMA system, which consists of eight electromagnetic coils, can steer the MR-SCS to the desired location in 3D space through the use of independent current control of the coils. As a magnetic propulsion test of the MR-SCS, the velocity of the MR-SCS was measured according to the gradient magnetic fields and fluid viscosity (Fig. 3). Fluid viscosity was determined to be within the range of 20 cP–60 cP, which is similar to that of the viscosity of the synovial fluid in the human body with an inflammatory reaction [22, 23]. Glycerol solutions were prepared as a synovial mimicking solution at several concentrations (70%, 75%, 80%) exhibiting different viscosities (fig. S9, Supporting Information). As shown in Fig. 3b, regardless of the fluid viscosity, the velocity of the MR-SCS

parsing_res_list.block_content: Journal Pre-proof

linearly increased at fixed magnetic field (40 mT) and changing gradient magnetic fields (0.9 - 1.8 T/m, 0.3 steps). Additionally, its velocity along the z-axis was slower than the velocity along the x-axis, as the moving MR-SCS in the working fluid was influenced by gravitational force. Considering variations in the fluid viscosity, the moving speed of the MR-SCS was observed to decrease proportionally as the fluid viscosity decreased. The results of this experiment verified the magnetic propulsion ability of the proposed MR-SCS. Additionally, in the articulated environment, gravitational force and fluid viscosity are important factors that act in addition to the magnetic field generated by the EMA system to affect the movement of the MR-SCS.
To assess the mobility of the MR-SCS using the proposed EMA system, 3D locomotion of a single MR-SCS was conducted in the 70% glycerol solution to mimic the conditions of synovial fluid (Fig. 3c and video S1, Supporting Information). In this test, for stable manipulation of the MR-SCS in 3D space, gravitational force was compensated for through the use of magnetic fields generated by the EMA system. As shown in Fig. 3c, the MR-SCS underwent an upward motion that could overcome gravity (Fig. 3c-(I)), and the levitated MR-SCS could then move along the rectangular path on the xy plane (Fig. 3c-(II-V)). While traveling on this path, the MR-SCS was steered through the teleoperation control at fixed 40 mT of the magnetic field and 1.4 T/m of the gradient magnetic field. The results of this experiment confirmed that the proposed MR-SCS is capable of 3D manipulation by the external magnetic field generated by the EMA system. Incidentally, the magnetic force of the MR-SCS is sufficient to overcome the gravitational force and the viscosity of the joint synovial fluid.

In an in vivo and clinical tests, the general optical imaging device is not easy to be used for real-time position tracking of MR-SCS due to the narrow space of the joint cavity in the knee joint. Therefore, we performed the feasibility test of real-time imaging of MR-SCS using x-ray and arthroscope devices, which are the representative imaging methods for observing knee cartilage. First, An X-ray fluoroscope can be adopted in the position tracking of the spheroid. This X-ray imaging makes it possible to observe the injection and targeting of MR-SCS into the joint cavity in real time through the high human permeability of X-ray (Fig. 3e). This characteristic of the x-ray imaging enables the non-invasive operation of knee cartilage regeneration using the proposed MR-SCS. To confirm the availability of the X-ray imaging, we performed a magnetic manipulation test of MR-SCS using an EMA system under a real-time X-ray fluoroscope. As shown in Fig. 3f and video S3 of Supporting Information, MR-SCS is visible through x-ray imaging and can be moved in the desired direction through magnetic field control by the EMA system. As another alternative, an arthroscope, which observes the insides of the knee and hip joints with small incision through the skin and other tissues, can be applied to view the micro-scaffolds in real time (Fig. 3g). Despite the small incisions for the arthroscope approach, the arthroscope are most often used because they provide a clear and direct view of the cartilage state of the knee joint and are free of unstable factors such as radiation exposure of x-ray fluoroscopes. Therefore, arthroscope imaging can be an effective way to observe the delivery of spheroids. Although the proposed EMA system demonstrated the precise 3D targeting delivery of a small number of MR-SCSs, cartilage defects with large size require a large number of MR-SCSs to be injected and manipulated into the joint cavity to fill cartilage defects. In addition, the proposed EMA system can generate magnetic fields along the same direction in the workspace. Hence, the MR-SCSs that spread to the synovial fluid can only move in the same direction and thus cannot be manipulated individually. For this reason, unlike manipulating a small number of spheroids, it is difficult to deliver a large number of spheroids injected into synovial fluid to cartilage defects. As an effective method for delivering large numbers of spheroids to cartilage defect with large size, we introduced a direct spheroids delivery method. In this method, the needle is located close to the defect, and the MR-SCSs injected through this needle move directly to the defect along the magnetic field generated by the EMA system. The delivery of MR-SCS by magnetic field using the EMA system showed a high efficiency of  $ 94.67 \pm 5\% $, which is superior to manual operation ( $ 18.50 \pm 18.35\% $). Here, the low performance of manual operation is caused by the fluid vortex formed around the defect by injection of the MR-SCS. To evaluate the effect of LF-EMF stimulation on chondrogenic differentiation of the MR-SCS, immunohistochemical staining of collagen type II and qRT-PCR were performed after LF-EMF stimulation for 30 min each day for 3 weeks. The confocal images of immunohistochemical staining of collagen type II indicated that LF-EMF stimulation did not affect chondrogenic differentiation of MR-SCS. For mRNA gene expression, expression of all chondrogenic gene markers, including collagen type II, SOX9, and Aggrecan, in MR-SCS after EMF stimulation was higher than levels observed without LF-EMF stimulation. 4. Discussion

In this study, we proposed a novel means of cartilage repair that uses MR-SCS combined with EMA and EMS systems. As a non-invasive surgery, intra-articular injection of stem cells to treat injured cartilage is being actively studies by a number of research groups [24, 25]. A large amount (2.5 × 10⁶ cells/500 μL/cm²) [26] of cells, however, is required to obtain cartilage regeneration effects due to the absence of active targeting. Intra-articular injection of these large amounts of cells can cause side effects such as the creation of loose body scar tissue on the surface of articular cartilage. Recently, several research groups have studied the targeting of MNP-labeled stem cells to lesions using magnetic fields as a means of overcoming these problems [27]. Most of these magnetic stem cell targeting studies, however, were evaluated using magnetically labeled individual cells and a bulky superconducting electromagnet [28]. Indeed, MNP concentration within the single magnetic cell is lower than that of the cell spheroid type, and this creates the need for a powerful EMA system. These single bulky magnet systems could not show 3D actuation and precise targeting of cells into defect sites. Given these shortcomings, in this study, we designed stem cell-based 3D spheroids that were composed of millions of MNP-labeled MSCs to improve magnetic propulsion and targeting accuracy without any cytotoxicity. It is known that MNPs internalize within cells through several endocytosis pathway, such as such as phagocytosis, micropinocytosis, clathrin- or caveolin-mediated endocytosis, then confine them into lysosomes, and degradation occurs [35, 36]. The properties of MNPs, such sizes, coating materials, charges, etc., are factors that determine the uptake pathway and uptake efficiency of the MNPs. The MNPs size smaller than 200 nm and charged MNPs showed higher rate of cellular uptake compared with MNPs size larger than 200nm, uncoated MNPs and neutral MNP [36, 37]. As already mentioned above, FluidMAG-D is coated with starch, size about 100 nm, and negatively charge on surface which meet all three conditions that increase uptake efficiency of the MNPs on cells. After the cellular uptake, MNPs degraded by the decrease in cell magnetization and appear in ferritin form. Internally, cells store the iron in ferritin form which is iron storage protein and release them during the degradation without toxicity [38]. Most well-known mechanism of cytotoxicity induced by MNPs on cells is production of excess reactive oxygen species (ROS) [39]. When excessive ROS produced by internalized MNPs can lead to cell apoptosis and necrosis. Iron ion (II) release from MNPs internalized in cells can cause the ROS production via reaction with hydrogen peroxide. However, if MNPs degraded and appeared in ferritin form, it will inhibit the ROS production and shows less cytotoxicity [38, 40, 41]. ### 4. Conclusion

We developed a magnetoresponsive stem cell spheroid-based cartilage recovery platform that can precisely deliver stem cells using an EMA system and can stimulate these cells by LF-EMF to promote chondrogenic differentiation. The 3D structured MSC-based spheroids were successfully fabricated and magnetically actuated into the cartilage defect region using the EMA system. Additionally, the chondrogenic differentiation of the defect-internalized MR-SCS was actively induced under LF-EMF signal to promote enhanced cartilage tissue formation. Biographies

Amr Yoo received her B.S. (2010) degrees from the Dep. of Applied Bioscience and Biotechnology at Chonnam National University, Korea and M.S. (2013) degrees from Division of Agriculture, Food, and Natural Resources at University of Missouri, USA. She is now a research associate at Korea Institute of Medical Microrobotics (KIMIRo), Korea. Her research interests are biomaterials and micro/nanorobots for medical approaches.

Gwangjun Go received his B.S. (2013) and M.S. (2015) degree from the School of Mechanical Engineering at Chonnam National University, Korea. Currently, he is a Ph.D. candidate in the School of Mechanical Engineering at Chonnam National University and a researcher of Korea Institute of Medical Microrobotics (KIMIRo). His research interests are biomedical micro/nanorobots.

Kim Tien Nguyen received a B.S. degree in Dept. of Mechanical Engineering from the Hochiminh University of Technology and Education, Hochiminh, Vietnam, in 2012 and the M.S. degree in School of Mechanical Engineering from Chonnam National University, Gwangju, Korea, in 2015, where he is currently working toward the Ph.D. degree with Korea Institute of Medical Microrobotics (KIMIRo). His research interests include micro-actuator/robot, micro-manipulation for biomedical devices/applications.

Kyungmin Lee received her B.S. (2013) and M.S. (2018) degrees from the Dept. of Mechatronics Engineering at Korea Ploytechnic University and Mechanical Engineering of Chonnam National University, Korea, respectively. She is now a Ph.D candidate in the School of Mechanical Engineering at Chonnam National University, Korea and a researcher of Korea Institute of Medical Microrobotics (KIMIRo). Her research interests include micro-actuator/robot, micro-manipulation for biomedical devices/applications.
